speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. You were talking about arriving at Brnenec to work for Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain to me what it was like when you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Brnenec, so I start right away on the machines what he had there prepared. Beside this, I had the machines what I took from Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And other three, four friends, what they knew also, the-- the-- the mechanical work, we start to put on the machines on the right spot, because we're supposed there to build, you know, to make the rockets, what we throw-- you know, the round one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grenade.

speaker: interviewee
text: The grenades-- mixing up. We supposed to produce grenades. But the machines what Schindler had prepared, they were not in good condition, and they were so huge, so big, we couldn't move with them, you know? And we were working very hard. Even sometimes he told us, slow down a little bit. There's time. You will do it a little bit later.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, and whenever he had a chance, you know, he was coming through and throwing cigarettes. He just put one cigarette and throw it away. Again, in a second, the other cigarette. He could throw 50, 60 cigarettes, you know? The people should pick up. They shall have a cigarette. They did it, you know, special, because he behaved like a father there to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every move, he figured out, you know, how to do it. So when we had a problem with the machines, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I had to look-- they were-- part of machines, they were prepared to work, and they were working on it, but we couldn't make, you know, the amount what we supposed to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after two months, you know, the Gestapo from Krakow send him a letter. I was very close with him. If he wanted to, we-- we shall know something. So we were five people. So at night, he used to come up, 12:00 on the night shift, and called us in the corridor and to tell us what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He received a letter that because we are not delivering the amount what we supposed to, and there are 1,300 people working. the Gestapo will send-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Gestapo is-- was sending food, and that's for nothing. You know, they are not giving us what they're supposed to. So he told us, and then they said, you should be prepared. He got-- they're gonna one day, they're gonna come over, and they will, all the 1,300 people, they're gonna kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, when he told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1944? '44 and '45, in the beginning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he told us this. So he says, listen, but here, have a look on the left. There is a basement. The basement is loaded with ammunition-- all kind of ammunition you have there. And across the street from our factory, we saw people was-- were preparing graves for us. That was, you know, in such bushes. And we saw they are going straight and preparing the graves. So he said, it will come one day, they will call you out, like for appell, you know? They will not tell you what for they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you should know, I will not be here. You should know, if they will call you, don't ask any questions. Run right away in the basement, and take any kind of ammunition, and you start the first. You shoot to the SS, what they will come from, the Gestapo. So this way, some people from you, they will survive. Otherwise, if you will not do this way, so all of you will be gone. So you can imagine, that's what we had, what kind of feeling, that we knew that one day will come. But we didn't know, really till today, we didn't know what kind of person he was, this Schindler. He find a way, and he find some money, I don't know from where. Maybe some people from Krakow, what-- what they were-- the rich ones, he didn't told us. And he went to a factory, the same like ours, but they were producing a lot, you know? They had new machines. And he went to the factory and he bought the amount what he supposed to send in.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means this was our production. We did it. And he send it. He didn't tell us this. He send it straight to the place where it's supposed to go. And he received a letter, or they called him by phone. They didn't show us the letter. The first letter, yes, but this one, no. So most probably they called that they received. And they are very satisfied, because the amount is good and the production is a very good one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he called us after, when he had the news, that we gonna work as long as possible, you know, like all the other factories, what they work for the Wehrmacht, for the German army. So you know, we couldn't tell this everything. We knew, the-- the five of us, we knew about it. But to tell to 1,000 men and 300 women what's going on, it would be impossible, you know, because they would-- they wouldn't go out from bed. They would cry, anyhow, they're going to kill me. So we, the five, we knew about it. And after the war, we told them, you know, what was it here, what-- what he prepared for us, Schindler. So with the food, you know, we didn't get from the Gestapo from Krakow. We didn't get food, or maybe some minimal that it was impossible to live on it and to work. He find out that the partisans, they are not far from the bushes there around. And whenever the German army is passing by, they run out from the holes there on horses, and they used to fight to kill some Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans, they killed them with the horses together. So Schindler find out that there are-- not far from the factory-- that there are killed horses. He took-- because they were people, Polish people from Krakow, not Jews, what they used to go out to do something, you know, to prepare. So he took three, four boys from them, and the horses and a big buggy, and he used to come with three horses, killed horses, on the buggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had people, they were real good cooks. They took out, you know, like a cow after when she's killed, you know? They took off the-- the-- the skin from it. And they were cooking in the kitchen. You know, we had a soup, because that was-- we had the meat, so we had the soup. And we had almost two pounds bread for 24 hours. So you can imagine that we had meat. We had the soup and a slice of bread. So we were really, you know, we were praying to God that it happened, you know, such-- you know, it's-- it's really, really hard to describe what a human being was able to do for us to keep us alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were working still. But sometimes at work, something happened, you know, with a finger that we put somewhere, and we were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were bleeding, the hands, or something fall down on a leg. So he made a big room, and four doctors were with us. And he was running for everything what was important to have in a room for the people, because they were checking in the morning, who needed, you know, this [INAUDIBLE], you know, this special kind if something happened that we had to keep the hand on this special kind. OK, everything we had what we needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the doctors were working for us. Sometimes somebody had a fever, so we had the other basement. They put it down, they shouldn't be with those healthy people. And we did really everything, whatever he-- he didn't ask that much, you know? We were doing really a lot, but this, what he did for us, it was more than possible. Impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was-- that was the life there. And he find a bakery, you know, where we used to get additional bread. We shall not be very hungry, you know, because he did really the most what he could. And that-- that was it until May-- May the 8th, May the 8th. But May the 7th--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this you're talking--

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. May the 7th, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he asked-- he was asking us that a few from us supposed to go with him because he has to run away. He is a German, you know, and he had papers as a Gestapo. So it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were a few. I didn't go with him. So it was three boys and three girls. They went with him because he knew that the Russians are already on the way to come to the factory. And he was afraid they gonna kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took the truck-- the Jewish boys-- and they went with him at night till the first station of the Russian officers there. And they came, and they introduced him. This is Mr. Schindler. What we are here-- we were here with him-- we, in Brnenec, were only with him nine months, nine, 10 months, but he had the factory before, so he had 500 people was working there for him also. You know, he used to deliver some other-- he was a mine factory. And he took over from one of the Jews what was the owner. And he was working there with all his family, you know? So till that time, they were working with him for at least three, three and a half years, because they went from the other factory straight to Brnenec. So they told this to the Russians, what he did for us. And they are 1,300 people, what they survived only because of him. They want to kill us many times. And we were in Gross-Rosen, and we were in Auschwitz, and somehow, he was running after the people until he get-- until he get all of the 1,300 people together, you know, in the factory. So he was our angel. So the Russians took him and they gave him a paper. You are free. We know that you were in the German, in the Gestapo, whatever, but you were working with Jews, and we-- we know that there is a camp. And tomorrow, we send a soldier to this camp, to-- because they are free already. And now, those people what came with you, those Jewish people, they can go back. Nothing will happen to them. And it was like-- like they said. He got that, and he went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right away, he went there where the Jewish people were concentrated, you know, the free people. So next day, like I say, the 8th of May, '45, came a soldier on a horse. And he starts to talk in Russian. So we didn't know much. I understood a little bit, but to talk with him-- he wanted to tell us everything, you know? You are free. The war is over. Hitler is killed, and the whole army, the Germany army, everything is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't say-- he was sitting, and the women, they were kissing his, you know, his legs. They are kissing the horse. And finally he says, listen, you are, all of you are Jewish. Let's talk Jewish. He was a Jewish soldier. Then we start to talk, and he told us, you are free. Everything belongs to you. You can go and [INAUDIBLE] whenever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did go. Where to go? There were some people, they went-- you know, they were Germans. They were loaded with food. They run into the house. They just throw them out. The Germans had to go out. But they grabbed the food, and they ate a lot of food. A few passed away, because, you know, the amount of food what they put in, the stomach couldn't take it. We were not used to eat that much. And you know, butter, and-- and whatever they-- they-- they had, food with salami, with-- with fat, everything. So we had a few, they passed away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I forgot to mention to you-- in the meantime, while we were working there, and we knew what's going on in the world, because he told us, they sent a train with 180 Jews, and with the SS. The first, they start-- they start to walk, and then they gave them a train but no food. When they started to walk, they were not able to walk. So from the 180 left-- was left 60. And the SS was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came with the train not far from our place, from Brnenec. And he find out there's a train with those people, and he said, nobody wants to take them. Where to go? Just to kill them, that was the only solution. He said, stop. I take all the people here. Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler. He came to the SS, to the car, to the train car, and he took over the 60 people. But it was impossible, you know, to touch those people. They were, you know, dead. Just, they-- they-- they-- they could-- you know, their eyes were open, but they were almost dead. We started to take them-- Schindler told us, take him on your arm, and we have the special barrack there. We will put them. And when all of them will be there, you will take them to shower up and to wash them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it was impossible, you know? Bone and skin, nothing else, you know, the people. But we were working on those people quite a few days. And-- it's here, I have it. Schindler's wife, she used to prepare special soups twice a day. She used to cook in her kitchen for those people. They should start to come up. They used to get from our food, but she, from where she had, you know, the additional soup, twice a day, such big, you know, dishes. And they were separated from us. They had their barracks to live. And he was the one that he-- additional 60 Jewish men, he-- they survived because of Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after the war, when this Jewish soldier, the Russian one, saw, you know, there were people-- they did-- they was able to do. So they asked the soldier, listen, we have here eight SS people what they-- they killed on the way going here before they had the train. They killed more than 100 Jews. So he said, you don't have to ask me. You do with them whatever you want. You are allowed to do whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, in my imagination, I knew what they had in mind to do with them. I left for a day. And I came after a day, day and night, you know, out somewhere to a house. It was other few like me. They were not there anymore, you know, because, you know, how many they-- they killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had some people between us, they were able to do something. I didn't ask. I didn't want to know all the details about it. But he gave them the permission. And he said, if you want to go to the city, you can take over any apartment you like it, because we are all over with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want-- well, and I-- in my mind, came to me, I said, what I going to look? What I going-- you know, I wouldn't be able to kill somebody. To hit somebody, it was very hard for me, you know? If somebody wants me to hit, then I was a little bit stronger, so I had, you know, for my person to do it. He-- he shouldn't do it. But to kill-- the worst one what I saw-- what I-- I saw when they were-- when they killed people, innocent people. If they would tell me, now, take over the gun, and you kill them. I wouldn't be able. This is my nature. You know, unfortunately, I am very soft. I can't-- I-- I-- I-- I wasn't able. So I decided that I want to go to Krakow. It was not very far from there to Krakow, and to see if somebody survived from my family. This is still 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So what did I do? I went to the bushes, and I grabbed two horses, and I grabbed a wagon. And I had people, what they were working, you know, to put on, on the horses, you know, when you want to put the horse to the-- so you have to put in, the special such from leather, what is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was two boys, they were working on this kind. So I told them, listen, I have two horses. You want to go also to Krakow. So prepare whatever you can. So then one of them bring other horse, a third horse, and we were preparing ourselves. So we had the two horses and the buggy, and we took a few girls with us, you know, what I knew them. They used to live close to me in Krakow. And those people, and we started to go to Krakow. On the way, we-- for a night, we stopped, you know? And Schindler, before he left, he-- he run away, it was a factory of shirts and suits. And they were working, the Czechs, there. In the same, you know, around our place, when we were working. So when they run away from there, and they left all the merchandise, Schindler, before he left, he took quite a few people to cut for all of us, for three suits, for six shirts, and underwear, and he gave us parcels, you know? We shall have with us, because we were poor. We were in the-- the-- the-- the suits what we used to wear in the-- in the camps. They were with the stripes. So we came to Krakow. We came and the Czechs, you know, they took us very nice of everywhere. They gave us space to-- to sleep, and for the horses and food for the horses. And we used to go, you know, three days. And we come to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Krakow, it was terrible. I find out nobody is here, my  family. I knew. I didn't expect much. But I was counting maybe some miracle happened, that somebody survived. But unfortunately, I was the only one. And I find those, my two friends, the husband and wife what I took their daughter through, you know, from the ghetto out. So they were-- they were earlier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because in Poland, it was three months earlier that-- what they left-- the-- the Jews in, you know, three months earlier. So they were already, and they took me to them. I shall live with them. I had our house what the parents left, but I went with them, and since that time, we were together.
